StatusProcess_AssessmentReferences: NA
StatusProcess_ExpertRecommendations: During the July 2025 expert review meeting; concern was expressed surrounding low benchmark values for the South-Thompson Bessette Creek CU; which may not accurately reflect the potential productivity and carrying capacity of the system. In addition; improving the genetic baseline for South-Thompson Bessette Creek to better distinguish Bessette fish from Middle Shuswap Summer 0.3 Chinook was suggested. Another research priority identified was to investigate and quantify sources of mortality by life stage.

Spn_EstSource_Specific: Local implementation of SBC Ck data processing code.
Spn_CalcMethod_Specific: Various survey types at multiple sites
Spn_CalcMethod_Notes: Combination of visual survey methods and high precision methods. Deadman River within the Lower Thompson CU was enumerated primarily by a fixed-site census from 1985-2024. A resistivity counter was added to supplement fence counts in 2000. Louis Creek was enumerated by visual survey methods using a peak live count expansion factor between 2005-2024 and by a fixed-site census using a weir from 1998-2004. Bonaparte River data were collected using a resistivity counter supplemented with downstream visual counts from 1996-2024. Nicola River data were collected using a Mark-recapture from 1995-2024 and historical data (1975-1995) have since been calibrated to become equivalent to the mark recapture method.
DataUpdate_ProcessDescription: NA
Rel_LowerBM_VersionRationale: Used 20p and 40p of Smax benchmarks because they tend to be more biologically conservative than MSY based ones; while still being generally consistent with the intent of upper and lower benchmarks under the WSP and in their application to status assessments across salmon CUs in the Pacific Region.
Rel_LowerBM_MethodNotes: For Bonaparte River (46206); Louis Creek (46598); and Deadman River (46216) used Parken et al. (2006) method to estimate Ricker parameters from watershed area. For Nicola River used Ricker spawner-recruit fit from Fraser Spring 1.2 FSRR (in review). CU-level benchmarks are the sum of site-level estimates.
Rel_UpperBM_VersionRationale: Used 85p of Smsy for consistency with other Chinook status assessments.

CU_Verification_Notes: Upper Adams River SU 1.x Chinook were extirpated by the presence of an impassable splash dam that was built on the lower Adams River in 1908 (Roos 1991). After dam removal in 1945; a new Chinook population was introduced using transplanted fish from other locations. It is unclear if this population is self-sustaining; therefore it has not been verified as a new CU. Area staff recommend changing this to EXTIRPATED; but formal Wade et al. (2019) review hasn't been completed. Original CU is extirpated. There is potentially a new CU in this spawning area.

Site_Verification_Notes: CSAS paper approved; Grant et al. 2011 https://waves-vagues.dfo-mpo.gc.ca/library-bibliotheque/344553.pd
AvgGen_Notes: selected based on average age of maturity; 85% of years are 4sub2; late 1970's some years had much lower 4sub2 though small number of fish in these years could be sampling error and average age of 4sub2 dropped to 78% . some years post-1970 low four year old proportion relative to age five in some years; however; this could be due to low abundances reducing accuracy of estimates?
Spn_EstType: effective total adult spawners (excludes jacks) for abundance metrics; effective female spawners for trend metrics
Spn_EstSource_General: DFO Fraser-BCI Stock Assessment Sockeye Analytical staff
Spn_EstSource_Specific: Trend metrics: DFO Fraser-BCI Stock Assessment Sockeye Analytical code that selects and treats the data used for trend analyses; Abundance metrics: data from stock-recruitment (production) database used by both the Pacific Salmon Commission and DFO. Sums all adult data and then multiply the total spawners by female spawner sucess.
Spn_CalcMethod_General: sum of spawning ground surveys either total adults or total females multiplied by spawner success (is calculated as the proportion of eggs (0%; 50%; or 100%) successfully spawned; based on spawning ground carcass surveys).
Spn_CalcMethod_Specific: high-precision fence counts (1950-1963). Visual surveys (1964+). Not sure if a 1995 calibration was applied to update escapement estimates 1964+.
Spn_CalcMethod_Notes: Escapement enumeration methods varied from largely fence counts in the earlier time series (1950-1963) to largely visual surveys (helicopter) from 1964 to present; with no gaps in the time series (Appendix 2). In 1995; a fence was installed to re-evaluate the expansion factor used to calibrate the visual surveys in this system. It was found that the expansion factor appropriate for this system (2.9) is much higher than that typically used for Fraser Sockeye (1.8). Therefore; previous surveys (1985-1994) may underestimate true escapement (Schubert 2007).
Site_Records_Source_Specific: Fraser-BCI Fraser Sockeye escapement database; NuSEDs often not up to date
DataUpdate_ProcessDescription: Area staff prepare input data (requires data input); then run data processing code.
Rel_LowerBM_VersionRationale: Method recommended in Holt 2009
Rel_LowerBM_MethodNotes: Bayesian estimate of Ricker model parameters. Last update of estimates used data for 1950 to 2010 brood years.
ActiveInterventions_History: Hatchery transplants were introduced into the Bowron system from Lakelse Lake (Skeena River hatchery) between 1924 and 1926 (Aro 1979). Since these transplants were not successful; population expansion within this CU after the Hells Gate landslide is likely attributed to remnant Bowron-ES Sockeye (Withler 1982).
Metric_Notes: can use all metrics: absolute; relative abundance and trend metrics
StatusProcess_AssessmentReferences: No publications yet but documented in data summaries on github
StatusProcess_ExpertRecommendations: update benchmarks estimates; consider Smax BMs as new assessments are moving to 20% and 40% Smax as more in alignment with Indigenous values; more robust to changes in productivity which is occuring for many CUs.

Spn_CalcMethod_Specific: visual surveys of single site: Portage Creek (1965+)
Spn_CalcMethod_Notes: Only Portage Creek was included in the escapement time series and no gap filling was required (Appendices 1 & 2). This system was assessed using visual survey methods (peak live-cumulative dead) throughout the time series. Data prior to 1954 is quite sporadic; therefore; only the time series from 1965 to present (after hatchery transplants from the lower Adams River became established in the Seton system) was used in the assessment of status. This time series reflects this newly established population post-hatchery intervention.

ActiveInterventions_History: details in Grant et al. 2011; transplants early on after extirpation of original population from Birkenhead and Lower Adams (Aro 1979). Genetically similar to Lower Adams river. No evidence of genetic bottlenecks. No current hatchery intervention. Run timing shifted to Late Timing; from original population that was Early Summer.

For Stock-Recruitment time series for BM uses escapement data from 1965+ starting when channel was built for apples-to-apples.
Spn_CalcMethod_General: sum of spawning ground surveys either total adults or total females multiplied by spawner success (is calculated as the proportion of eggs (0%; 50%; or 100%) successfully spawned; based on spawning ground carcass surveys).
Spn_CalcMethod_Specific: Weaver Creek; Weaver Channel; and East Creek. Mark recaptures 1950-1988; with some years assessed using peak live cumulative dead methods; and channel diversion fence at the Weaver Creek channel
Spn_CalcMethod_Notes: Three sites are included in the escapement time series: Weaver Creek; Weaver Channel and East Creek and no gap filling was required (Appendices 1 & 2). East Creek has independent data early in the time series but was included in the Weaver Creek estimate after 1951. Steelhead Creek is not included separately in the escapement records; and has also been rolled up into the totals for Weaver Creek. Weaver Creek and Channel cannot be evaluated independently; since numbers of Sockeye in each is a consequence of channel loading regimes at the diversion fence located at the outlet of this system. Data for the channel begins in 1965 after its construction. From 1950 to 1988; mark recapture surveys were primarily used to assess escapement into Weaver Creek (with the exceptions of 1951; 1966-1968; which were assessed with peak live cumulative dead methods). From 1989 to 2009; peak live cumulative dead visual surveys were conducted (with the exceptions of 1994; 1996 and 1998; which were assessed using mark recapture methods; and from 1999-2000 and 2002-2003; which were assessed using an enumeration fence). Weaver Channel was exclusively assessed at the channel diversion fence; using counts of live Sockeye migrating above the diversion weir to the spawning channel; the upper creek; and into the ESSR holding channel. Fish removed for ESSR were not counted in escapements. Visual surveys were conducted in lower Weaver Creek. downstream of the diversion fence; and carcass surveys were conducted upstream and downstream of the diversion fence
Site_Records_Source_Specific: Fraser-BCI Fraser Sockeye escapement database; NuSEDs often not up to date

ActiveInterventions_Notes: Hatchery channel drives production in this system. This is included in the time series to evaluate status. Given differences in channel loading any differences in abundance b/w channel and creek are based on decisions on loading. So for assessment purposes you cannot separate out the channel from the creek.

Sums all adult data and then multiply the total spawners by female spawner sucess.
Spn_CalcMethod_General: sum of spawning ground surveys either total adults or total females multiplied by spawner success (is calculated as the proportion of eggs (0%; 50%; or 100%) successfully spawned; based on spawning ground carcass surveys).
Spn_CalcMethod_Specific: single site Birkenhead River various methods
Spn_CalcMethod_Notes: Only the Birkenhead River was included in the escapement time series (Appendix 1). The Birkenhead River has been consistently assessed throughout the time period; and makes up over 99% of the escapement in years when other populations were also assessed. All other populations comprise only a minor component of total production for the Lillooet-Harrison-L CU; and these populations have only been opportunistically assessed with lower precision methods (visual ground surveys). Birkenhead River was assessed with a mark recapture program up to 1999. Biases in the mark recapture methods were identified in 1994; and methods were modified in 1995. Conclusions of a 1995 study indicated that the pooled Petersen population estimates were no longer significantly biased (Schubert and Tadey 1997; Houtman et al. 2000). In recent years; assessment methods have varied. Specifically; in 2000 an overflight visual survey was used to assess Sockeye escapements; in 2001 a counting tower was used; and subsequently (post-2002) an enumeration fence has been used to assess escapement. The year 2002 represents the only gap in the Birkenhead escapement time series when Sockeye escapements were not assessed. This

Site_Adj_Notes: The year 2002 represents the only gap in the Birkenhead escapement time series when Sockeye escapements were not assessed. This year was gap filled using the average of the previous and subsequent generation (cycle-line average methods)(Appendices 2 & 5).

Therefore; only Big Silver Creek is included in the escapement time series and no gap filling was required (Appendix 2).

Spn_CalcMethod_General: spawning ground surveys total females multiplied by spawner success (is calculated as the proportion of eggs (0%; 50%; or 100%) successfully spawned; based on spawning ground carcass surveys).
Spn_CalcMethod_Specific: Two sites were included in the escapement time series: Nahatlatch Lake and Nahatlatch River (Appendix 1). The river assessments began in 1975 using visual surveys (peak live cumulative dead methods). Consistent lake assessments began in 1980; using standard visual survey (lake expansion) methods; there are a few years of sporadic data prior to 1980; but assessments are less reliable and did not use systematic methods
Spn_CalcMethod_Notes: Two sites were included in the escapement time series: Nahatlatch Lake and Nahatlatch River (Appendix 1). The river assessments began in 1975 using visual surveys (peak live cumulative dead methods). Consistent lake assessments began in 1980; using standard visual survey (lake expansion) methods; there are a few years of sporadic data prior to 1980; but assessments are less reliable and did not use systematic methods. The Nahatlatch Lake estimates were gap filled for the years 1975; 1976; and 1978 using the Mean Proportion Method (gap filled values were calculated using the proportional contribution of the lake to the system using only years with assessments for both lake and river sites) (Appendices 2 & 5). In 1979 the lake estimate is included in the river abundance estimate and; therefore; gap filling for this year was not required.

Spn_CalcMethod_General: spawning ground surveys total females multiplied by spawner success (is calculated as the proportion of eggs (0%; 50%; or 100%) successfully spawned; based on spawning ground carcass surveys).
Spn_CalcMethod_Specific: Taseko Lake is a glacially influenced lake that has; as a result; poor fish visibility. Carcass counts are expanded based on survey effort; using methods established from studies historically conducted on Taseko Lake. Estimates are likely biased low given limitations in the number of carcasses that reach the lake surface after becoming moribund (Patterson et al. 2007b). Lake counts can be further compromised on survey days with heavy rain or winds that decrease the visibility of carcasses on the lake surface.

Freshwater_Adaptive_Zone: STh + Nth + Lth + LFR + MFR + FRCany + LILL
Spn_EstSource_Specific: Fraser River Panel Annual Report Data - Shiny App
Spn_CalcMethod_Specific: Estimates evolved from sum of spawning ground surveys to mainstem mark recapture; then mainstem test fishery; and now mainstem sonar.
Spn_CalcMethod_Notes: Estimates before 1993 are based on the sum of spawning ground surveys for major populations. Estimates starting 1993 are based on returns to Mission minus any harvests above Mission. There is nuSEDS data; but it isn't used for the status assessment. nuSEDS has 96 POP_ID assigned to Odd-year Fraser Pink. For most there are sporadic records; for a few there are persistent records for extended time periods. Most records are entered as total returns to river rather than natural spawners. POP_ID 44551 ("Fraser River and Tributaries") is for a Petersen mark-recapture estimate for the whole system at Mission from 1993-2001. After that system-wide estimates developed from a PSC test fishery (2003-2007) and starting in 2009 from the PSC sonar at Mission. PSC estimates since 2003 are not in nuSEDS. The full time series of system-wide estimates is maintained by the PSC. There was no calibration for changing methods over time but Glaser et al. (2025) state that "based on conversations with area staff and other analysts familiar with the data; were assumed to account for the vast majority of the spawning population in any given year; and to not be systematically biased." Res doc points to Grant et al 2014 for details. No description of any recent developments? Table 1 of the res doc lists method by time period.
Spn_Coverage_Category: All?? (But can’t be used for abs abd? Why?; How handle?)
Site_Adj_Notes: Not using site-specific estimates since 1993. Earlier data has been reviewed and aggregate estimate bilaterally adopted through PSC; so time series is not linked to any site-level data.
DataUpdate_ProcessDescription: NA
Rel_LowerBM_VersionRationale: Used default benchmark when appropriate SR model fits available.
Rel_LowerBM_MethodNotes: State-space implementation in STAN of AR1 Ricker model (i.e. lag-1 autoregression)
Rel_UpperBM_VersionRationale: Used default benchmark when appropriate SR model fits available.
ActiveInterventions_Specific: Release of unfed and/or fed fry
ActiveInterventions_Notes: Since the 1980s there have been annual releases of up to 2.5 M fed or unfed fry from Chehalis and Chilliwack hatcheries. Recent production plan have higher targets (e.g. 6.7 M in 2023). At about 2percent survival rate; this translates into about 135k hatchery-origin adults out of a recent 10year average return around 7.4 M (less than 2 percent of return)
ActiveInterventions_DataAdjustmentsNotes: Hatchery contribution to total CU is very small; so no adjustment needed.
StatusProcess_AssessmentReferences: NA

CU_Verification_Notes: CU definition was reviewed during 2024 status review process and revised to include 2 additional rearing lakes (i.e. from Osoyoos to Osoyoos-Skaha-Okanagan). This change was reviewed and formally approved through the Wade et al. (2019) process and  corresponding revisions were implemented in regional databases (e.g. nuSEDS).
Site_Verification_Notes: Detailed habitat surveys available to map all spawning areas and associated survey sites.
Spn_EstSource_Specific: ONA spawner database (any public link?Zenodo?)
Spn_CalcMethod_Specific: Sum of AUC estimates based on visual spawning ground surveys covering all known spawning sites
Spn_CalcMethod_Notes: Consistent surveys with mutiple visual incpections and AUC estimate starting in 2002; but CU has been recolonizing additional rearing lakes so not using all available estimates. Status assessment uses the sum of spawner estimates for Osoyoos Lake and Skaha Lake starting in 2012. 2010 and earlier were excluded because the Skaha component of the series didn’t have spawners yet. 2011 was excluded because the spawner estimate for Skaha that year is considered highly uncertain. A consistent time series for the sum of Osoyoos and Skaha spawners is available starting in 2012. Once a spawning population in Okanagan Lake is established; the CU level time series needs to be revisited. nuSEDS has 1 pop_ID (1864; Okanagan River) with estimates covering 1997-1999. WHERE TO GET LIST OF SITES COVERED BY SURVEYS?
DataUpdate_ProcessDescription: NA
Rel_LowerBM_VersionRationale: Used spawning habitat capacity estimates as the basis for biological benchmarks; because (1) SR model fits for the Osoyoos Lake population performed poorly and sufficient data are not available at this time to fit SR models for the Skaha Lake population; and (2) estimates of lake rearing capacity were considered too uncertain due to data only from a short time window and no data for recent years.
Rel_LowerBM_MethodNotes: Estimated spawners that maximize recruitment (i.e. full use of available spawning habitat) based on detailed spawning ground surveys (gravel size; gradient; water level on 0.1 by 0.1 m grid) over multiple years with most recent surveys in 2023.
ActiveInterventions_Notes: Fry released into creeks and river segments above each of the 3 rearing lakes (Osoyoos; Skaha; Okanagan). Only Osoyoos and Skaha have self-sustaining spawning populations at this time. Both have high PNI (Above 80p most years).
ActiveInterventions_DataAdjustmentsNotes: High PNI; so chose to use all natural spawners for the time series.
StatusProcess_AssessmentReferences: NA

ActiveInterventions_Notes:
Whitehorse Rapids Fish Hatchery started in 1984 as mitigation for turbine induced mortalities of migrating Chinook salmon juveniles. Since 1988; a hatchery component of returning Chinook has been documented. There is currently an annual release target
 675 of 150;000 smolt 0+ Chinook for rebuilding purposes.Estimated based on fin clips and various tagging prorgrams; the proportion of hatchery-origin spawners (pHOS) in the Whitehorse component of the CU is highly variable; but frequently accounts for the majority of spawners in that population.
ActiveInterventions_DataAdjustmentsType: Exclude estimated share of hatchery-origin fish
ActiveInterventions_DataAdjustmentsNotes: Excluded annual estimated pHOS from spawner series for the Whitehorse component. Majority of natural-origin spawners is from Takhini population.
StatusProcess_AssessmentReferences: Connors et al. (In Press) Res Doc; DFO (In Press) SAR; Connors et al. (2025); Res Doc Supplement with WSP status details on Xenodo.
StatusProcess_ExpertRecommendations: Genetic baseline should be improved for populations downstream of Lake Laberge to resolve the boundary between this CU and the Middle Yukon CU (CK-73); and to improve the CU assignment of fish counted at the border. Increasing the collection of consistent tributary level data could help to inform a more accurate biological status and allow for more timely recognition of changes in status.

Spn_EstSource_Specific: Bayesian Run Recon Model (ability to update annually????)
Spn_CalcMethod_Specific: Used hierarchical Bayesian integrated run reconstruction and spawner-recruit model with data for 1985-2024. Run recon combined various survey estimates and genetic stock ID.
Spn_EstUnc_Details: CU-level spawner estimates from Bayesian run reconstruction come with posterior distribution for each annual estimate. Time series for status assessment used the median of the posterior.
DataUpdate_ProcessDescription: TBD
Rel_LowerBM_VersionRationale: Used 20p and 40p of Smax benchmarks because they tend to be more biologically conservative than MSY based ones; while still being generally consistent with the intent of upper and lower benchmarks under the WSP and in their application to status assessments across salmon CUs in the Pacific Region.
Rel_LowerBM_MethodNotes: Used hierarchical Bayesian integrated run reconstruction and spawner-recruit model with data for 1985-2024 returns to calculate Ricker (no time varying productivity) parameters and associated biological benchmarks. Median posterior estimates used for the status assessment. Estimates incorporate sonar based estimates of spawner abundance post 2005. Reconstructions in non-sonar years are based on combined Middle Mainstem and Big Salmon CU run reconstruction (with uncertainty); because genetics cannot reliably differentiate the two; and proportional contribution (with uncertainty/variability) of Big Salmon in years with sonar estimates.
StatusProcess_AssessmentReferences: Connors et al. (In Press) Res Doc; DFO (In Press) SAR; Connors et al. (2025); Res Doc Supplement with WSP status details on Xenodo.
StatusProcess_ExpertRecommendations: Given declining trends; status for this CU should be tracked annually. Increasing the collection of consistent tributary level data could help to inform a more accurate biological status and allow for more timely recognition of changes in status.
